359	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
360	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
361	
362	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
363	
364	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
365	
366	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
367	
368	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
369	
370	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
371	
372	## Dependency graph
373	
374	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
375	
376	```
377	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
378	```
379	
380	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
381	
382	## Stage transitions
383	
384	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
385	
386	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
387	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
388	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth proper
9	geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis.
10	End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms. **Char-general:** the
11	protected signatures take `[Field k]` only — NO `CharZero`. Any char-0
12	sub-case route is therefore PARTIAL only.
13	
14	The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally); only
15	`isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. Spine:
16	**pointed vs. unpointed**. `genus C := \dim_k H^1(C, O_C)` (arithmetic
17	genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).
18	
19	## Phases & estimations
20	
21	LOC bands honored per-row velocity. A.1.a's realized ~50 LOC/it is the
22	EASIEST sub-phase; deeper substrate (Stacks 052H, Quot/Grassmannian,
23	codim-1) plausibly runs at ~10–25 LOC/it because each iter must first
24	invent missing Mathlib API. Velocity figures in
25	`LOC (remaining · realized/it)`. Cell-internal arithmetic: `remaining ÷
26	realized` should roughly match `Iters left`.

28	| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |

11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.

40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and

49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, ~10 LOC)

54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `IdentityComponent G : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme, not the
60	  tautological `G` itself.
61	- `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme G` — asserts the *existence* of an
62	  open immersion morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` whose underlying map of
63	  schemes is both an open and a closed immersion (clopen subscheme).
64	- `Pic0Scheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme.
65	- `PicScheme.degree C : (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ` —
66	  a genuine function from `k`-points to `ℤ`, not a constant.
67	- `Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety C` — asserts the conjunction of the four
68	  abelian-variety properties (proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible,
69	  group-object structure); not vacuous because each conjunct is a genuine
70	  property/structure on the (typed-sorry) `Pic0Scheme C`.

94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/
109	
110	namespace GroupScheme
111	
112	/-- The **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
113	finite type.
114	
115	Encoded as a `k`-scheme: an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))`, carrying the
116	intended substantive identity "this is the connected component of `|G|`
117	containing the identity section `e`, equipped with the open-subscheme
118	structure inherited from `G`". The associated open immersion
119	`IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` is the content of
120	`IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`.
121	
122	iter-186+: the body constructs the identity component as the open subscheme
123	of `G` whose underlying topological space is the connected component of `|G|`
124	at the image of the identity section. The construction uses
125	`ConnectedComponents.mk` on the underlying topological space, then the
126	open-subscheme structure (EGA I 6.1.9: locally Noetherian spaces have open
127	connected components). For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed
128	`sorry`. -/
129	noncomputable def IdentityComponent {k : Type u} [Field k]
130	    (_G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
131	    [GrpObj _G] [LocallyOfFiniteType _G.hom] :
132	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
133	  sorry
134	
135	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
136	
137	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
138	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
139	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
140	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
141	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
142	subscheme).
143	
144	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
145	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
146	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
147	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
148	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
149	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
150	
151	iter-186+: the body constructs the inclusion morphism via the open-subscheme
152	structure of `IdentityComponent G`, applies EGA I 6.1.9 (locally Noetherian
153	⟹ open connected components) for the open-immersion conclusion, and applies
154	the "closure of a connected subspace is connected" elementary argument for
155	the closed-immersion conclusion. For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is
156	a typed `sorry`. -/
157	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
158	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
159	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
160	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
161	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} :=
162	  sorry
163	
164	end GroupScheme

166	/-! ## §2. The identity component of the Picard scheme
167	
168	We specialise the abstract identity-component substrate to
169	`G = PicScheme C`, the Picard scheme of a smooth proper geometrically
170	integral curve `C/k` (from sibling `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`).
171

175	namespace Scheme

177	/-- The **identity component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.
178	
179	Defined as the identity component
180	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` of the Picard scheme
181	`Pic_{C/k}` (from sibling `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`,
182	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`). By
183	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`, `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an
184	open and closed subgroup scheme of `Pic_{C/k}` of finite type over `k`,
185	geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with extension of the
186	base field.
187	
188	iter-186+: the body unwinds to `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)`
189	once the `[LocallyOfFiniteType (PicScheme C).hom]` instance lands on
190	`PicScheme C` (it follows from the Kleiman §4 structure: `Pic_{C/k}` is
191	locally of finite type as the disjoint union of open quasi-projective
192	`k`-subschemes). For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
193	noncomputable def Pic0Scheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
194	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
195	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
196	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
197	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
198	  sorry

200	/-! ## §3. The degree map
201	
202	The disjoint-union structure of `Pic_{C/k}` (a disjoint union of open
203	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via
204	`PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient`) stratifies its `T`-points by the leading
205	coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
206	relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation. On `k`-points this gives the
207	**degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
208	
209	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree_pic` (Milne III.1, p.~88). -/
210	
211	namespace PicScheme
212	
213	/-- The **degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
214	
215	Sends a `k`-point `λ ∈ Pic_{C/k}(k)` --- a morphism
216	`Spec k ⟶ (PicScheme C).left` --- to the leading coefficient of the
217	Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf `L` on `C` (relative
218	to a fixed degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`). By Riemann--Roch,
219	`χ(C, L ⊗ O_C(n)) = n · deg L + 1 - g`, so the degree is the leading
220	coefficient of `Φ_L(n)`, well-defined on the isomorphism class `[L]` and on
221	the `k`-point `λ` (because `PicScheme C` represents the étale-sheafified
222	relative Picard functor).
223	
224	The degree map is a group homomorphism for the additive structure on
225	`Pic_{C/k}(k)` (tensor product on `L`) and the standard `(ℤ, +)`. The full
226	group-homomorphism refinement / functoriality in `k` lives as a follow-up
227	lemma in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the underlying function.
228	
229	iter-186+: the body extracts the representing invertible sheaf from
230	`(PicScheme.representable C)`, forms its Hilbert polynomial via the project's
231	Hilbert-polynomial machinery (sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`), and returns
232	the leading coefficient. For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed
233	`sorry`. -/
234	noncomputable def degree {k : Type u} [Field k]
235	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
236	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
237	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
238	    (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ :=
239	  sorry
240	
241	end PicScheme
242	
243	/-! ## §4. `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety
244	
245	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies `Pic⁰_{C/k}` with an
246	abelian variety of dimension `g(C)` --- the Jacobian variety of `C`. In
247	this project, "abelian variety" is the conjunction of the four properties
248	`[GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]`
249	threaded through `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity` and consumed by
250	`AlgebraicJacobian.Albanese.AlbaneseUP`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety` (Kleiman §5
253	Ex.~`ex:jac` + Rmk.~`rmk:Jac`; cf. Milne §I.1, Rmk. III.1.4(e)). -/
254	
255	namespace Pic0Scheme
256	
257	/-- **`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety.**
258	
259	For `C/k` a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus `g = g(C)`
260	over a field `k`, the identity component `Pic⁰_{C/k}` of the Picard scheme
261	is an *abelian variety over* `k`: a smooth, proper, geometrically
262	irreducible `k`-group scheme. (Commutativity follows automatically from
263	Milne §I.1, Cor. 1.4, and is not separately stated here.)
264	
265	The bundled statement: the four pieces `[IsProper] ∧ [Smooth] ∧
266	[GeometricallyIrreducible] ∧ Nonempty (GrpObj _)` hold on
267	`(Pic0Scheme C).hom` and `(Pic0Scheme C)` respectively.
268	
269	Dimension `g = g(C)`: not stated separately at the file-skeleton level
270	(it requires `dim_k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C`, which
271	involves the Krull-dimension API on the underlying scheme); iter-186+
272	follow-up.
273	
274	iter-186+: the body assembles the four conjuncts from
275	- `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` (clopen subscheme of
276	  `PicScheme C`),
277	- Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p` (`X/k` projective + geom. integral ⟹
278	  `Pic⁰_{X/k}` quasi-projective; upgrade to projective when geom. normal,
279	  which holds for smooth proper curves of positive genus),
280	- Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Ex.~`ex:jac` (smoothness of `Pic_{X/k}` at the
281	  identity for smooth proper curves; hence smooth of dimension
282	  `dim_k H¹(C, O_C) = g(C)` everywhere by uniform smoothness on the open
283	  identity component).
284	For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
285	theorem isAbelianVariety {k : Type u} [Field k]
286	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
287	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
288	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
289	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧ Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
290	      GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
291	      Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C)) :=
292	  sorry
293	
294	end Pic0Scheme
295	
296	end Scheme
297	
298	end AlgebraicGeometry

11	% (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} +
12	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is new project material:
13	% the Lean target

16	%
17	% NOTE: future split candidate when a second consumer materialises. The
18	% abstract identity-component substrate is kept in this chapter (rather
19	% than split off into a separate
20	% \texttt{GroupScheme\_IdentityComponent.tex}) because it is small and

22	% revisit the split.
23	
24	% NOTE (planner directive iter-184). The Lean target file

28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94	
95	\begin{theorem}\leanok
96	[Identity component is an open subgroup scheme of finite type]
97	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
99	  
100	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
101	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
102	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
105	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
106	  % extending \(k\)."
107	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
108	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
109	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
110	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
111	  subscheme of \(G\), the open immersion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a
112	  homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes (in particular \(G^0\) inherits a
113	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\)), \(G^0\) is of finite type over
114	  \(k\) and geometrically irreducible, and the formation of \(G^0\)
115	  commutes with extension of the base field: for any field extension
116	  \(K/k\), the natural map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is an isomorphism.
117	\end{theorem}
118	
119	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
120	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with

120	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
121	% (3).)
122	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
123	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
124	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
125	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
126	% \(G^0\) is open too.
127	%
128	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
129	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
130	% extending \(k\).
131	%
132	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
133	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
134	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
135	%
136	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
137	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
138	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
139	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
140	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
141	% [\dots]
142	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
143	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
144	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
145	% [\dots]
146	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
147	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
148	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
149	\begin{proof}
150	  
151	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
152	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
153	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
154	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
155	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
156	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
157	  the open-subscheme structure.
158	
159	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
160	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
161	  particular the formation of \(G^0\) commutes with extension of the
162	  base field. The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is again connected
163	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
164	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
165	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
166	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group operation
167	  (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
168	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes.
169	
170	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
171	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
172	  commutes with field extension). Over \(\overline k\), the product
173	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
174	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
175	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
176	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
177	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
178	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
179	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
180	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
181	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
182	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
183	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
184	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
185	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
186	\end{proof}
187	
188	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
189	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
190	
191	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
192	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
193	
194	\begin{definition}\leanok
195	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
196	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
198	  
199	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
200	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
201	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
202	  % L2921-L2929).
203	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
204	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
205	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
206	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
207	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
210	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
211	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
212	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
213	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
214	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
215	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
216	  % \(k\).
217	  %  \end{prp}"
218	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
219	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
220	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
221	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
222	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
223	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
224	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
225	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
226	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
227	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
228	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
229	  extension of the base field.
230	\end{definition}
231	
232	\section{The degree map}
233	\label{sec:pic_degree}
234	
235	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
236	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
237	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
238	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
239	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
240	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
241	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
242	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
243	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
244	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
245	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
246	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
247	degree.
248	
249	\begin{definition}\leanok
250	[Degree of a Picard class]
251	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
253	  
254	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
255	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
256	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
257	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
258	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
259	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
260	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
261	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
262	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
263	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
264	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
265	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
266	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
267	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
268	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
269	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
270	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
271	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
272	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
273	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
274	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
275	  \[
276	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
277	  \]
278	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
279	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
280	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
281	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
282	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
283	  \[
284	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
285	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
286	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
287	  \]
288	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
289	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
290	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
291	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
292	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
293	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
294	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
295	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
296	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
297	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
298	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
299	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
300	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
301	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
302	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
303	  of degree \(d\).
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
307	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
308	
309	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
310	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
311	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
312	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
313	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
314	automatically.
315	
316	\begin{theorem}\leanok
317	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety of dimension \(g\)]
318	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
320	  
321	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
322	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
323	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
324	  % L3990-L3996).
325	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
326	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
327	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
328	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
329	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
330	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
331	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
332	  % \end{ex}"
333	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
334	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
335	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
336	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
337	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
338	  % Exercise~REF."
339	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
340	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
341	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8) and Rmk.~III.1.4(e),
342	  p.~86 (dimension equals genus).}
343	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
344	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). Then the identity component
345	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an
346	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)}: a smooth, proper, geometrically
347	  irreducible commutative \(k\)-group scheme. Its dimension is
348	  \[
349	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g \;=\; g(C),
350	  \]
351	  the genus of \(C\) (\cref{def:genus}). Its \(k\)-points are exactly the
352	  classes of invertible sheaves of degree zero:
353	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\), the kernel of the degree map of
354	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.
355	\end{theorem}
356	
357	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
358	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
359	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
360	% identity-component substrate.)
361	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
362	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
363	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
364	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
365	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
366	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
367	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
368	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
369	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
370	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
371	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
372	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
373	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
374	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
375	% everywhere."
376	% (from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86:)
377	% "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
378	% then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
379	% when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
380	% \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
381	% from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
382	\begin{proof}
383	  
384	  We assemble the conclusion from four inputs.
385	
386	  \emph{Open and closed subgroup of finite type, geometrically
387	  irreducible.} By
388	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
389	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
390	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
391	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
392	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
393	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
394	  separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
395	  and connected: it is a complete subscheme \emph{candidate} for the
396	  abelian-variety conclusion.
397	
398	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
399	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
400	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
401	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
402	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
403	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
404	
405	  \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper geometrically
406	  integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is also geometrically
407	  normal (the smoothness implies geometric regularity, hence geometric
408	  normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p then upgrades the
409	  quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
410	  projective over \(k\), in particular proper. The proof of th:qpp\&p
411	  uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling
412	  out non-constant maps \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a
413	  geometrically normal \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
414	
415	  \emph{Smoothness and dimension.} By Kleiman~\S 5, Cor.~cor:sm,
416	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g\)
417	  (\cref{def:genus}), with equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is
418	  smooth at \(0\), in which case it is smooth of dimension \(g\)
419	  everywhere. For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the Picard scheme is

734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}
790	
791	\section{Lean encoding}
792	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
793	
794	The Lean target file is the new
795	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
796	proceeds in three phases.
797	
798	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
799	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
800	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
801	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
802	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
803	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
804	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
805	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
806	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
807	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
808	
809	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
810	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
811	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
812	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
813	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
814	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
815	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
816	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
817	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
818	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
819	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
820	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
821	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
822	
823	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
824	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
825	Grassmannian
826	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
827	\begin{enumerate}
828	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from
829	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
830	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
831	  \item the Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) from
832	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
833	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
834	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
835	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
836	    stratum \(T_0^\Phi\);
837	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
838	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
839	    projective \(S\)-scheme.
840	\end{enumerate}
841	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
842	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
843	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
844	
845	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
846	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
847	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
848	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
849	full \(R^i f_*\) statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
850	pinned commit.
851	
852	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
853	This file directly consumes

851	
852	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
853	This file directly consumes
854	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
855	gluing infrastructure and
856	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
857	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
858	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
859	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
860	\(E = \mathcal{O}_C\) on a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), giving the Hilbert
861	scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
862	and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective divisors.
863	
864	\section{Out of scope}
865	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
866	
867	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-scheme existence statement and
868	its proof skeleton via the Grassmannian + flattening-stratification
869	route. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
870	\begin{itemize}
871	  \item \textbf{Flattening stratification} (A.2.a) -- consumed-by, not
872	    proved-here. Its blueprint home is the new chapter
873	    \texttt{Picard\_FlatteningStratification.tex}.
874	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
875	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
876	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot scheme and is
877	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
878	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford \(m\)-regularity theorem} -- input to
879	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}, not proved here. The proof lives in
880	    Nitsure \S 2; for the Lean encoding it is a project-side sub-build.
881	  \item \textbf{Semi-continuity and base change} (Nitsure \S 3) -- the
882	    flat-base-change theorem of cohomology (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology})
883	    is stated here as a lemma, but the upstream Mathlib bridge / project-side
884	    sub-build is not constructed in this chapter.
885	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
886	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
887	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
888	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
889	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
890	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
891	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
892	  \item \textbf{Hironaka's counterexample to representability of
893	    \(\Hilb_{X/\mathbb{C}}\) in the non-projective case} (Nitsure \S 1,
894	    Exercise (8)) -- pedagogical only; Route A operates entirely in the
895	    projective setting.
896	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
897	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream of the Quot existence
898	    statement; its blueprint home is
899	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
900	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
901	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
902	    constructed in this chapter.
903	\end{itemize}
904

input.description: List subagent descriptors and check progress-critic directive shape

content:
blueprint-reviewer.md
blueprint-writer.md
lean-auditor.md
lean-vs-blueprint-checker.md
mathlib-analogist.md
progress-critic.md
refactor.md
reference-retriever.md
strategy-critic.md
---
name: progress-critic
description: Fresh-context audit of recent iteration progress per active file/route. Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-going-in-circles, and *throughput drift* (this iter's progress is much slower than STRATEGY.md's estimate predicts). Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.
write_domain: "task_results/**"
read_only: true
can_spawn: false
default_enabled: false
mandatory: [plan]
dispatcher_notes: |
  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
    do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
    have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
    verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.

    **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
      - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
        or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
        skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
      - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
        signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
        added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
        CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
      - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
        sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
        is no trajectory to extrapolate from).

    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
    with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
    open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
    re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
  - My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
    progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
    judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
    corrective.

  ### Strict context discipline

  Your directive must contain ONLY:
    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
      iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
    - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
      sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
      (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
    - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
      and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
      so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
      K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
      `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
      whole strategy.

---
name: blueprint-reviewer
description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
write_domain: "task_results/**"
read_only: true
can_spawn: false
default_enabled: false
mandatory: [plan]
dispatcher_notes: |
  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
    has to throw away.
  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.

    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
        run);
      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
        currently under active prover work;
      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
        dropped from objectives).

    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
    chapter view is the point of running me.
  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
---WRITER---
---
name: blueprint-writer
description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
read_only: false
can_spawn: true
default_enabled: false
dispatcher_notes: |
  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
    multi-route coverage.
  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
    what the directive lists.
  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
    agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
    writer with TWO globs:
      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
---REFACTOR---
---
name: refactor
description: Execute structural Lean changes (definitions, signatures, file splits, imports) under plan-agent direction. Inserts `sorry` at broken proof sites; never fills proofs.
write_domain: "**"
read_only: false
can_spawn: true
default_enabled: false
---

# Refactor Agent

You are the refactor agent. You modify definitions, signatures, types, and imports across all `.lean` files under plan agent direction. You can also create/delete/divide files under the plan agent's guidance.

## Your Job

The plan agent has identified a structural problem and written a directive describing what to change. Your invocation prompt tells you the directive's location (read it from disk) and the slug for this invocation. The directive contains:
- **Problem**: what's wrong
- **Mathematical justification**: why the change is correct
- **Changes requested**: exact replacements for each definition/signature
- **Affected files**: where cascading breakage will occur
- **Expected outcome**: what the sorry landscape should look like after

Read the mathematical justification carefully — it tells you the intent behind each change, which you need when fixing cascading type mismatches in downstream files.

## Slug

Your invocation prompt contains a line `Slug: <slug>`. Use it for the report filename: `.archon/task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`. Multiple refactors per iteration are allowed and each uses a distinct slug, so do not write to the unsuffixed `task_results/refactor.md`.

When you yourself were dispatched from a parent subagent (`ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` is set), your report lands at `.archon/task_results/<parent-slug>/refactor-<slug>.md` — the Archon CLI handles the path automatically and your invocation prompt names the exact path.

## Problem
<what is structurally wrong>

## Mathematical Justification
<why the change is correct, in enough detail that you can fix cascading type mismatches>

## Changes Requested
- File: <path>
  - Old: <signature or definition>
  - New: <signature or definition>

## Affected Files
<list of files expected to break>

## Expected Outcome
<what the sorry landscape should look like after>
```

## Rules

### Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface, **no agent may modify their signature**. As the refactor agent, under the directive you may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. You cannot do any other modification to `archon-protected.yaml`.

### Blueprint-based informal content

This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof sketches live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:

```
Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
```

(The blueprint slug refers to the per-file chapter name, not the refactor invocation slug — the two are unrelated.)

### What you CAN do
- Modify any `.lean` file: definitions, signatures, types, imports, module structure
- Create new `.lean` files or delete existing ones
- Delete false or wrong declarations
- Change quantifier ordering in lemma statements
- Add new definitions, structures, or type classes
- Insert `sorry` at proof sites broken by your changes

### What you MUST do
- **Keep all files compiling.** After every change, check compilation with `lean_diagnostic_messages`. If a change breaks downstream proofs, insert `sorry` at the broken sites. The prover will fill them later.
- **Follow the plan agent's directive exactly.** Do not improvise beyond what was requested. If you think additional changes are needed, document them in the "Notes for Plan Agent" section of your report but do not make them.
- **Document every change** in `task_results/refactor-<slug>.md` (see Logging below).
- **Verify the full project compiles** before finishing. Use `lean_diagnostic_messages` on every file you touched plus files that import from them.
- **Ensure that the Lean files reflect the blueprint structure.** The plan agent gave you the directive and updated the blueprint with the intended structure. Your job is to make the necessary changes to the Lean files to match that structure.

### What you MUST NOT do
- **Do NOT fill proofs.** If a proof breaks because you changed a definition, insert `sorry` and move on. Proof filling is the prover's job.
- **Do NOT edit PROGRESS.md, task_pending.md, task_done.md, or USER_HINTS.md.**
- **Do NOT make changes unrelated to the directive.**
- **Do NOT modify the names or signatures of protected declarations listed in `archon-protected.yaml`.** You may move them to a different file, but not rename or re-sign them.
- **Do NOT modify the blueprint chapters.** The plan agent updates the blueprint with the intended informal structure and markers; your job is only to make the Lean files match that structure.
- **Do NOT exceed your declared write-domain.** Your invocation may have been launched with `--write-domain <glob>...`; you cannot write to Lean files outside those globs. Children you spawn must declare write-domains that are strict subsets of yours.

## Workflow

1. Read the directive file pointed to by your invocation prompt
2. Read the **Mathematical justification** section — understand why each change is correct
3. Read the blueprint chapters corresponding to the affected files to understand the intended structure and how the changes fit into it
4. Read the affected `.lean` files to understand the current state
5. Plan your changes: list which files need modification and in what order (modify definitions first, then fix downstream consumers)
6. Execute changes file by file, checking compilation after each file
7. Handle cascading breakage: when changing a definition in file A breaks file B, fix the type signatures in B and insert `sorry` at broken proofs
8. Verify compilation across all affected files
9. Write your report to `task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`

## Spawning child subagents (optional)

For large refactors that naturally decompose into independent file-level pieces, you may dispatch child subagents via the generic wrapper. To discover what is available this iteration: `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter.

Dispatch pattern (Bash; treat as blocking — await each child's report before acting on it):

```
python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
  --name <subagent-name> \
  --slug <child-slug> \

input.description: Ensure iter-186 logs dir + survey analogies

content:
# Analogy: `carrierSet → Submodule → presheaf → sheaf` API alignment for `lineBundleAtClosedPoint`

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api

## Iteration
185

## Question

The project has been blocked for ~18 iters on completing the
`carrierSet (Set K(C)) → carrierSubmodule (Submodule kbar K(C)) → presheaf →
isSheaf → Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` chain inside
`RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean` for the Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` direct
construction of `lineBundleAtClosedPoint`. The iter-185 progress-critic
verdict is CHURNING + OVER_BUDGET. The directive asks four questions:

1. Is there a Mathlib idiom for "subsheaf of `K(C)` cut out by an order
   condition" that the project should ALIGN to (vs. building stepwise)?
2. What is the right Mathlib API for the three `Submodule` closure proofs
   (`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`)? Is the project's `Scheme.RationalMap.order`
   aligned to it, or parallel to it?
3. Should the sheaf property go via stalk-locality (the iter-183 plan)
   or via the constant-sheaf-restriction `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
   pattern (the project's existing `toModuleKSheaf` template)?
4. Is `Scheme.RationalMap.order` (`WithZero.log ∘ Ring.ordFrac`) a
   parallel API to Mathlib's `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation`,
   and should the project align?

## Project artifact(s)
- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean:153-166` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet` (iter-183 axiom-clean `Set`).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean:181-192` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono` (iter-183 monotonicity).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean:224-233` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` (typed `sorry` body).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:152-156` — `Scheme.RationalMap.order` (project's `WithZero.log ∘ Ring.ordFrac` ℤ-valued projection).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:173-186` — `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` carrier class (currently exports only `Ring.KrullDimLE 1`).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean:32-44` — `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` + `toModuleKSheaf` (the project's existing `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`-construction template that the recipe will mirror).

## Decisions identified

### Decision 1 — `Subpresheaf`/`Subsheaf` API for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`-valued sheaves

- **Mathlib idiom**: There is **no** Mathlib subsheaf API at the right
  level. Concretely:
  - `CategoryTheory.Subpresheaf` / `CategoryTheory.Subfunctor`
    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Subfunctor.Basic`) is the standard idiom
    for "subsheaf cut out by a per-open predicate" — but it ships
    **`Functor C (Type w)`-only**. There is no `ModuleCat R`-valued
    subpresheaf machinery; `CategoryTheory.Subpresheaf` is parameterised
    over `F : Functor Cᵒᵖ (Type w)`, period.
  - `SheafOfModules.subobject` / dual / kernel functors do not ship.
    `SheafOfModules R` is over a sheaf-of-rings `R`, a different
    category from `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` — and even there, no
    subobject-by-per-open-submodule API exists.
  - The Type-valued `Subfunctor.sheafify_isSheaf`
    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Subsheaf`) computes the sheafification
    of a sub-presheaf but operates only on `Type`-valued targets.
- **Project's current path**: stepwise build (per-open
  `Set → Submodule → Functor → IsSheaf → Sheaf`), mirroring the
  project's existing `toModuleKPresheaf → toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf →
  toModuleKSheaf` template (`StructureSheafModuleK/{Presheaf,SheafProperty}.lean`).
- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent at the *direct-build* level. Mathlib
  ships the same building blocks (`ModuleCat.of`, `Presheaf.IsSheaf`,
  `Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`) but no shortcut for
  "subsheaf-of-`Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`-from-per-open-submodules".
- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** with the stepwise direct build — there is no

### Decision 2 — `Submodule` closure proofs (`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`)

content:
## Recommendation

### Verdict summary

| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| 1: Subsheaf-of-ModuleCat idiom | PROCEED | informational |
| 2a: Zero closure | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major |
| 2b: Add closure (+ class upgrade) | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | critical |
| 2c: Scalar closure | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major |
| 3: Sheaf property route | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | critical |
| 4: `RationalMap.order` API alignment | PROCEED | informational |

The route is **not** blocked by a missing Mathlib idiom or a parallel-API
trap. It is blocked by a single under-strength carrier-class field
(`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out` exports `Ring.KrullDimLE 1`
instead of `IsDiscreteValuationRing`) and by the iter-183 plan choosing
the harder of two sheaf-property routes (stalk-gluing instead of
forget-to-Type-then-trivial-on-irreducible-scheme).

### Concrete iter-186 recipe (5 steps, ~105 LOC total)

**Step 1 — Class upgrade** (~10 LOC, edit `WeilDivisor.lean:171-186`):

Replace the `out : ... Ring.KrullDimLE 1 ...` field with
`out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)`.
Add an instance synthesising `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` from
`IsDiscreteValuationRing` (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE` /
`IsDedekindDomain` chain provides this — DVR ⟹ PID ⟹ Krull-dim-≤-1).

Note: this affects the existing `Scheme.RationalMap.order` users
(`principal`, `principal_hom`, `principal_degree_zero`, the
`globalSections_iff_*` helpers, etc.), but all consumers currently take
`[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]` as a class hypothesis, so the
class-field rewrite propagates automatically without any signature change
downstream.

**Step 2 — `carrierSubmodule`** (~25 LOC, new declaration after
`carrierSet_mono` in `OCofP.lean`):

```lean
private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
  zero_mem' := by
    -- order Y 0 = WithZero.log 0 = 0; both `0 ≥ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold.
    refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
  add_mem' := by
    -- case-split on f + g = 0 (trivial via WithZero.log_zero), else
    -- apply Ring.ordFrac_add (DVR-shipped by Step 1) + WithZero.log
    -- monotonicity on the nonzero part.
    rintro f g ⟨hf₁, hf₂⟩ ⟨hg₁, hg₂⟩
    refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => ?_, fun hPU => ?_⟩
    · ... -- use `Ring.ordFrac_add` after the case-split
    · ... -- same pattern at P
  smul_mem' := by
    -- For c : kbar, algebraMap kbar (stalk) c is a unit when c ≠ 0;
    -- Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit gives order = 0; order Y (c • f) = order Y f.
    -- For c = 0, c • f = 0 reduces to zero_mem'.
    rintro c f ⟨hf₁, hf₂⟩
    ...
```

**Step 3 — Presheaf functor** (~30 LOC, new declaration):

```lean
private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
    (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar where
  obj U := ModuleCat.of kbar ↥(carrierSubmodule P hPcoh U)
  map {U V} f := ModuleCat.ofHom <|
    Submodule.inclusion (carrierSet_mono P hPcoh
      (CategoryTheory.leOfHom f.unop))
  map_id U := by ext ⟨x, _⟩; rfl
  map_comp _ _ := by ext ⟨x, _⟩; rfl
```

**Step 4 — Sheaf property via `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`** (~40 LOC):

Mirror `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` (`SheafProperty.lean:32-37`):

```lean
private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf_isSheaf
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
    Presheaf.IsSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
      (lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf P hPcoh) := by
  rw [Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget _ _
        (CategoryTheory.forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
  -- Now goal: the Type-valued forget of `presheaf P hPcoh` is a sheaf.
  -- The forget is `U ↦ ↥(carrierSubmodule U) ⊆ K(C)`.
  -- For each cover {Vᵢ} of U and matching family (fᵢ ∈ carrierSet Vᵢ),
  -- all fᵢ are equal in K(C) (overlap-matching + irreducibility), so
  -- f := f₁ ∈ K(C) is the unique extension; f ∈ carrierSet U because
  -- every Q ∈ U lies in some Vᵢ where the per-Q order condition holds.
  ...
```

The body uses `IsIntegral C.left → IrreducibleSpace C.left.toTopCat`
(Mathlib instance) to force "overlap matching ⟹ same `f ∈ K(C)`" via
density of nonempty opens; then the per-`Q` condition holds because
every prime divisor `Q ∈ U` is in some `Vᵢ`.

**Step 5 — Bundle** (~5 LOC, replace `OCofP.lean:224-233` `sorry`):

```lean
noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]   -- already in the namespace section
    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]  -- already in scope
    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=
  ⟨lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf P hPcoh,
   lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf_isSheaf P hPcoh⟩
```
I'll prepare all 5 subagent directives in parallel, then dispatch them.
